
- Observed result after fix: current PR head validates and doctors the intended memory role-slot matrix after direct upgrade from the last public release. Usable `github-copilot` auth survived rollback/upgrade testing and was confirmed by post-upgrade `models status` plus a live provider turn. Five clean source CLI agent turns and one exact-head gateway service-dispatched agent turn ran against `github-copilot/gpt-5.4-mini`. Gateway service proof required `gateway install --force`; a non-forced install left the already-installed `2026.6.1` unit in place, which was explicitly caught and corrected before claiming gateway proof.
- What was not tested: maintainer taxonomy approval is still a review decision, not something this proof can self-certify. Opengrep was not installed locally on Nano, so changed-path scan coverage remains GitHub Actions evidence. Runtime proof logs were redacted to avoid private host, path, token, session, and network values.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the old-release config validation failure is expected and captured as compatibility evidence for future PR-era slot keys; it is not a failed PR-head upgrade. The gateway service is loopback-bound on Nano and was tested through local gateway CLI calls.

## Implementation notes

- Adds `src/plugins/slot-resolution.ts` as the shared role-slot helper layer for canonical memory role keys, defaults, normalization, effective per-agent resolution, and configured selection collection.
- Updates config schema/types/help/labels/docs for global and per-agent memory role slots.
- Keeps `PluginSlotsConfig.contextEngine` for global plugin slots, and adds an agent-slot type/schema that omits `contextEngine`.
- Updates doctor legacy memory-slot handling so `doctor --fix` canonicalizes to `memory.recall` while retaining legacy `memory` during the compatibility window.
- Updates plugin loader/activation/startup resolution so explicit role-slot selections activate memory plugins without confusing default `memory-core` fallback with a user-selected plugin under restrictive allowlists.
- Extends validation to report missing role-slot plugin refs and validate schemas for plugins selected only by non-recall or per-agent memory roles.
- Extends skill publishing, status scanning, uninstall cleanup, memory runtime, dreaming, compaction provider, and SDK alias behavior to use the role-aware model.
- Keeps memory runtime registrations addressable by plugin id, so runtime recall dispatch follows the resolved global or per-agent `memory.recall` owner.
- Passes the session agent id into Pi embedded extension factory construction so safeguard compaction provider selection honors per-agent `memory.compaction` overrides in both normal session runs and manual/queued compaction paths.
- Updates bundled memory plugin manifests/docs to describe role support and the recall shorthand migration.

## Compatibility and risk

Backward compatibility preserved:

- Existing `plugins.slots.memory` configs continue to resolve as recall selection.
- `doctor --fix` now keeps the legacy `plugins.slots.memory` key in place after copying to `memory.recall`, so third-party plugins that still read only the legacy key do not hit an immediate flag day.
- The default recall plugin remains `memory-core` unless explicitly disabled or overridden.
- `plugins.enabled = false`, denylist behavior, and explicit `plugins.entries.<id>.enabled = false` remain respected.
- Global `plugins.slots.contextEngine` remains separate from memory roles and continues to select context engines such as LosslessClaw.

Compatibility changes reviewers should inspect:

- `memory.recall`, `memory.compaction`, `memory.capture`, `memory.dreaming`, and `memory.userModel` become durable public config keys after merge.
- Per-agent plugin-slot overrides are memory-only. `agents.list[*].plugins.slots.contextEngine` is intentionally rejected until a future PR defines and implements agent-scoped context-engine semantics.
- Legacy `plugins.slots.memory` is still deprecated as a canonical authoring target even though it remains supported and retained for compatibility.

Primary risks:

- The public memory role taxonomy becomes API surface after merge, so naming and docs need maintainer signoff.
- Role-gating mistakes could attach the wrong memory provider or suppress expected memory behavior; this PR adds focused coverage across loader, validation, startup, runtime, doctor, status, skill publishing, and cleanup surfaces.
- Third-party plugins should eventually read `memory.recall`, but the retained legacy key avoids forcing that ecosystem update immediately.

## Verification

Current PR branch head:

- `3f1f547042220aee42b3e39b8cb7ecb15cfac993` (`fix: honor disabled memory recall slot for startup embeddings`)
- Branch: `kklouzal:feat/multi-slot-memory-architecture`

target.title: feat(plugin-sdk): onModelDiagnosticEvent for trusted model.* events
target.body:
## Why

Plugins subscribing to diagnostic events via `onDiagnosticEvent` (from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/diagnostic-runtime`) never see the `model.*` lifecycle. The public API filter at [`src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts:879-886`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts#L879-L886):

```ts
export function onDiagnosticEvent(listener: (evt: DiagnosticEventPayload) => void): () => void {
  return onInternalDiagnosticEvent((event, metadata) => {
    if (metadata.trusted || event.type === "log.record") {
      return;
    }
    listener(event);
  });
}
```

drops **every** event whose dispatcher metadata is `trusted: true`. The runtime emits `model.call.started`, `model.call.completed`, `model.call.error`, `model.usage`, and `model.failover` via `emitTrustedDiagnosticEvent`. So the entire model lifecycle is invisible to plugins.

This breaks observability/cost-attribution plugins. A telemetry bridge plugin needs:

- `model.call.completed` (provider/model identity) to populate per-call span attributes
- `model.usage` (token counts, cost) for per-call attribution

Today both subscriptions are dead code against the trusted emit paths. Concretely, the embedded codex/Responses runner emits the full model lifecycle via `emitTrustedDiagnosticEvent` (see `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.ts`), but no plugin listener ever receives any of it — model traffic on the dashboard side ends up bucketed as "unknown" instead of `gpt-5.5`/etc.

## Approach

Add a focused, opt-in plugin-sdk API `onModelDiagnosticEvent` that exposes exactly the five model lifecycle event types — and only those — to plugins. The general `onDiagnosticEvent` filter is unchanged: it still drops trusted events, so the safety contract of the broad subscription is preserved. Plugins that want model telemetry have to opt in explicitly.

The new helper otherwise mirrors `onDiagnosticEvent`'s contract (cloned-and-frozen payloads, isolated listener failures, unsubscribe disposer).

A module-local allowlist (`PLUGIN_VISIBLE_TRUSTED_MODEL_EVENT_TYPES`) documents which event types are reachable through this API and is the single place to update if more model lifecycle types are added later.

## Files

- `src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts` — add `onModelDiagnosticEvent` + allowlist (+45 lines)
- `src/plugin-sdk/{diagnostic-runtime,index,compat}.ts` — re-export the new function so plugins can `import { onModelDiagnosticEvent } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/diagnostic-runtime"` (or the top-level barrel / compat subpath)
- `src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts` — three new tests:
  1. delivers all five `model.*` trusted types and filters non-model traffic
  2. the original `onDiagnosticEvent` still hides model events from plugins (defends the safety contract)
  3. unsubscribe disposer stops further delivery
- `CHANGELOG.md` — Unreleased / Changes entry

Total: 6 files, +158 / −2.

## Tests

- `src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts`: 22/22 passing
- Plugin-SDK guardrail tests (`src/plugins/loader-sdk-import-guardrails.test.ts`, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-root-alias.test.ts`): 44 tests, all passing

## End-to-end validation

Built a full `dist/` from this branch, swapped it into a deployed gateway, and ran a real codex (`openai-codex/gpt-5.5`) turn from a Telegram-driven session. Bridge plugin logs show **both** subscriptions on startup:

```

[agentweave-bridge] subscribed to model.* trusted events via plugin-sdk
```

…and during the turn the bridge now receives the `model.usage` event for codex/gpt-5.5 (previously dead-code path):

```

```

The downstream dashboard bucket that had been showing "unknown" for every codex turn now correctly attributes to `gpt-5.5`.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Plugins subscribing via the public `onDiagnosticEvent` API never observe trusted `model.*` lifecycle events (`model.call.started/completed/error`, `model.usage`, `model.failover`) because the existing filter at `src/infra/diagnostic-events.ts:879` drops every event whose dispatcher metadata is `trusted: true`. On my deployed install, this surfaced as Codex `gpt-5.5` turns bucketing as "unknown" in a downstream observability bridge plugin (agentweave-bridge) — the per-turn span never received `prov.llm.{provider,model}` because the bridge's `model.usage` / `model.call.completed` handlers were dead code against the trusted emit paths.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw v2026.5.10-beta.1 gateway running under `systemctl --user` on Linux (Debian 12, Node 24.13 via nvm) on my NAS (192.168.1.70), driving a Codex subscription session via Telegram channel and the embedded pi-runner. Local install built from this PR head: `dist/` produced by `pnpm build` from commit 52f87ce58a, swapped into `/home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist/`, gateway restarted. Bridge plugin installed under `~/.openclaw/user-plugins/agentweave-bridge/`, rebuilt against the new SDK export.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```
  # 1. Build the patched openclaw from this branch
  cd /home/Arnab/dev/openclaw-model-usage
  CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
  CI=true pnpm build

  # 2. Install over the canonical openclaw dist/
  systemctl --user stop openclaw-gateway
  mv /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist.bak
  cp -r dist /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist
  systemctl --user start openclaw-gateway

  # 3. Confirm trust-gated body in the built artifact
  grep -A2 "function onModelDiagnosticEvent" \
    /home/Arnab/clawd/projects/openclaw/dist/diagnostic-events-*.js

  # 4. Drive a real Codex turn through Telegram and observe the bridge plugin logs
  journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway -f | grep agentweave-bridge
  ```
- Evidence after fix:
  - Built-artifact contains the trust-gated callback:
    ```
    function onModelDiagnosticEvent(listener) {
      return onInternalDiagnosticEvent((event, metadata) => {
        if (!metadata.trusted) return;
    ```
  - Gateway startup logs from `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway` show the plugin subscribing to BOTH streams (previously only one):
    ```
    [agentweave-bridge] OTel SDK started, exporting to: http://192.168.1.70:30418/v1/traces
    [agentweave-bridge] subscribed to diagnostic events via plugin-sdk
    [agentweave-bridge] subscribed to model.* trusted events via plugin-sdk
    ```
  - During a real Telegram-initiated Codex turn (openai-codex/gpt-5.5), the bridge plugin now receives a trusted `model.usage` event that was previously invisible to it:
    ```
    [agentweave-bridge] started root span for main session: agent:main:main agent: nix-v1
    [agentweave-bridge] event: model.usage sessionKey: agent:main:main source: undefined
    [agentweave-bridge] ended root span for session: agent:main:main
    ```
- Observed result after fix: The downstream observability dashboard that had been bucketing every Codex turn as "unknown" model now correctly attributes them to `gpt-5.5`, with cost figures flowing through the bridge's pricing fallback. No behavior change for any other plugin: the broad `onDiagnosticEvent` subscription still filters every trusted event as before (defended by the regression test `onDiagnosticEvent still hides model.* trusted events from plugins`). A separate regression test confirms forged untrusted `model.*` events from `emitDiagnosticEvent` do NOT reach the new opt-in subscription.
- What was not tested: I did not exercise this against an isolated subagent (`agent:isolated:*`) session, only `agent:main:main`. Did not run on macOS or Windows; tested under Linux Node 24.13 only. Did not run the live `pnpm test` matrix exhaustively — the four review-required test files all pass locally (`src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts` 23/23, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-index.test.ts` 5/5, `src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-root-alias.test.ts` 13/13, `src/plugins/loader-sdk-import-guardrails.test.ts` 8/8) plus `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` and `pnpm build` are clean.

target.title: fix(memory): align session file counter denominator with indexer filter (fixes #77338)
target.body:
## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `openclaw memory --status` session file counter uses `.endsWith(".jsonl")` as the denominator filter, but the usage indexer in `src/infra/session-cost-usage.ts:413` uses `isUsageCountedSessionTranscriptFileName()` which excludes `.deleted.*`, `.reset.*`, `.bak.*`, and `.trajectory.jsonl` files. This mismatch inflates the session file count in the `--status` output.

- **Environment tested:** macOS 26.4.1, Node v22, OpenClaw built from source at `b554c470`.

- **Steps run after the patch:**
  1. Verified the bug still exists on current `upstream/main`: `grep -n 'endsWith.*jsonl' extensions/memory-core/src/cli.runtime.ts` → line 529 confirms the stale `.endsWith(".jsonl")` filter.
  2. Verified the correct filter is used elsewhere: `grep -n 'isUsageCountedSessionTranscriptFileName' src/infra/session-cost-usage.ts` → line 413 confirms the indexer uses the canonical filter.
  3. Applied the 2-line fix (import + filter replacement).

- **Evidence after fix:**

```
$ grep -n 'isUsageCountedSessionTranscriptFileName' extensions/memory-core/src/cli.runtime.ts
12:import { isUsageCountedSessionTranscriptFileName } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-host-engine-qmd";
530:      (entry) => entry.isFile() && isUsageCountedSessionTranscriptFileName(entry.name),

$ grep -n 'isUsageCountedSessionTranscriptFileName' src/infra/session-cost-usage.ts
13:  isUsageCountedSessionTranscriptFileName,
413:    .filter((entry) => entry.isFile() && isUsageCountedSessionTranscriptFileName(entry.name))
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The `scanSessionFiles` function now uses the same canonical filter as the usage indexer, ensuring `.deleted.*`, `.reset.*`, `.bak.*`, and `.trajectory.jsonl` files are excluded from the session file count denominator.

- **What was not tested:** Live `openclaw memory --status` output (requires full runtime setup with session transcripts directory). The fix is a direct alignment of the filter function — behavior is deterministic from the source code.

target.title: fix(gateway): render codex inline media once
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Codex-harness replies that emitted local `MEDIA:` image directives could fail to become trusted outgoing media, leaving inline images text-only or unavailable to WebChat/Control UI.
- Why it matters: Agents using the Codex harness should be able to return generated/local images inline without requiring channel-specific manual workarounds, and users should not see duplicate assistant turns for one image reply.
- What changed: Trust structured local media markers from non-external tool results, while preserving the existing registered-tool trust checks; replace the latest raw assistant `MEDIA:` transcript turn with the normalized media-bearing message instead of appending a second assistant image turn.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This does not change external MCP tool trust, local media root serving checks, model prompting, ComfyUI plugin behavior, or any provider/network API.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

No matching upstream issue was found. Searches checked: `inline image`, `inline media`, `Codex harness image`, `MEDIA: image`, `assistant-media`, `managed media`, `Control UI image`, and duplicate image/WebChat terms.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The Codex/AppServer path can persist the model-authored assistant reply as raw text containing `MEDIA:<local path>` while the gateway reply payload carries the parsed/normalized media URL. The WebChat transcript bridge treated those as separate assistant replies and appended the normalized media message after the raw directive turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing media tests covered parsing and directive stripping, but did not cover the Codex/AppServer split where the raw transcript text and normalized media payload represent the same reply.
- Contributing context (if known): Local media trust was tied to registered/built-in tool-name trust. Workspace tools that return a structured trusted local media marker needed a generic non-external trust path, still leaving external MCP results untrusted and downstream local-root serving checks intact.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.tools.media.test.ts`
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in:
  - Non-external structured `trustedLocalMedia` tool results keep local media URLs, while external tool results with the same marker do not.
  - A raw assistant transcript reply containing `MEDIA:<local path>` is replaced by the normalized outgoing media message when the reply payload carries equivalent media, instead of producing two assistant messages.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests hit the two affected seams directly: tool-result media trust filtering and WebChat transcript persistence/replacement.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing directive/media tests covered stripping `MEDIA:` directives into outgoing media blocks, but not replacement of a raw persisted Codex/AppServer assistant turn.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A; new regression coverage is included.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex-harness agents can render trusted local image replies inline in WebChat/Control UI without leaving a raw `MEDIA:` line behind or showing a duplicate text-plus-image assistant reply.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[Codex assistant MEDIA reply] -> [raw transcript assistant text]
                              -> [gateway normalized media reply appended]
                              -> [duplicate assistant turns]

After:
[Codex assistant MEDIA reply] -> [raw transcript assistant text detected]
                              -> [latest assistant turn replaced with normalized media blocks]
                              -> [single inline image reply]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

Security notes:

- Structured local media trust is ignored for external MCP tool results.
- Registered-tool trust behavior remains in place for built-in/trusted tools.
- The downstream managed-media pipeline still gates local file serving against allowed local media roots before exposing any media URL.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local development workspace; Windows gateway hot-patch was separately smoke-tested before PR preparation.
- Runtime/container: Node local workspace.
- Model/provider: Codex harness path observed with `gpt-5.5` during manual smoke testing.
- Integration/channel (if any): WebChat/Control UI transcript handling and Codex harness agent media payloads.
- Relevant config (redacted): Codex harness enabled; no secrets required for the local unit/seam regression tests.

### Steps

1. Run a Codex-harness agent reply that emits a local image as `MEDIA:<local path>`.
2. Observe the persisted assistant transcript turn and outgoing gateway media payload.
3. Open WebChat/Control UI for the same session.

### Expected

- The reply appears once as an inline image-bearing assistant message.
- The raw `MEDIA:` directive is not shown to the user.
- Local media from external tool results is not trusted solely because it carries a structured marker.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the raw `MEDIA:` assistant text could remain in the transcript and the normalized media message could be appended as a second assistant reply.
- Workspace tool local media markers were not accepted through a structured non-external trust path.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Passing local verification on the rebased branch:

```text
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo
# passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
# passed

node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.tools.media.test.ts
# 42 passed

OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PROJECT_SHARDS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts
# 132 passed
```

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - Rebased the fix onto current `openclaw/openclaw` `main`.
  - Verified the agent media trust tests pass.
  - Verified the gateway directive/WebChat media transcript tests pass.
  - Verified TypeScript core and core-test typechecks pass after the upstream rebase.
  - Earlier manual WebChat/Control UI smoke testing confirmed a Codex inline image rendered as one message rather than a duplicate text-plus-image pair.
- Edge cases checked:
  - External tool results with `trustedLocalMedia: true` remain untrusted for local media URLs.
  - Codex/AppServer raw path vs normalized `file://` payload equivalence is handled by replacing the latest raw media directive turn.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full `pnpm build` after the rebase, because this local shell did not have `pnpm` on `PATH`; targeted tests and core typecheck were run directly with repository scripts.
  - Fresh Windows Control UI visual smoke from this exact rebased commit; the equivalent runtime hot-patch was tested before PR preparation.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

N/A for initial PR submission.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Replacing the latest assistant transcript turn could hide an unrelated prior assistant message if detection is too broad.
  - Mitigation: Replacement only runs when the latest leaf is an assistant message containing parseable `MEDIA:` directives and the current reply payload is media-bearing; tests cover the raw Codex/AppServer directive replacement path.
- Risk: Structured local media markers could be abused by external tool results.
  - Mitigation: The marker is ignored for external MCP/tool results, and media serving still requires downstream allowed-root validation.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex/AppServer replies that emit a raw `MEDIA:<local path>` image now normalize into the existing assistant transcript turn instead of leaving raw MEDIA text and appending a duplicate gateway-injected media reply. Current head tested: a046242234bbbdc369730a38fc9b0b2d1be9d211.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw PR checkout on Linux, Node v24.13.1, real session transcript files, local media file fixtures, and the gateway chat transcript/media rewrite path.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: From the PR checkout after applying the ClawSweeper review fixes, ran `git diff --check`, `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH CI=true timeout 300s pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, and `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
$ PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH CI=true timeout 300s pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.6 /tmp/openclaw-pr-resolve-20260517

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  77 passed (77)
   Start at  00:34:46
   Duration  12.92s (transform 2.53s, setup 291ms, import 6.75s, tests 5.52s, environment 0ms)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts\n\n RUN  v4.1.6 /tmp/openclaw-pr-resolve-20260517\n\n Test Files  1 passed (1)\n      Tests  78 passed (78)\n   Duration  12.41s\n\n[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 23.72s
```

- Observed result after fix: The focused gateway transcript/media suite passed on current head, including the new parser-parity regression for an unquoted local MEDIA path containing spaces, coverage that preserves captions while stripping raw `MEDIA:` directives, refuses to rewrite an older matching media turn after a newer assistant turn, and avoids a second message-level transcript update after the file-level rewrite event. The browser proof rendered the normalized assistant image reply as a single bubble with one image and no raw `MEDIA:` text.
Browser-rendered Control UI proof on current head:

```text
$ node --input-type=module <Playwright proof runner>
{
  "screenshotPath": "/home/daein/.openclaw/workspace/logs/pr-watch/75727-ui-proof-2ce0267.png",
  "result": {
    "imageCount": 1,
    "bubbleCount": 1,
    "rawMediaVisible": false,
    "assistantText": "Here is the image."
  }
}
```

Proof setup: temporary Vite page rendered the actual Control UI renderMessageGroup implementation from this PR branch in system Chromium via Playwright. The message shape matched the normalized gateway result after the raw MEDIA transcript turn is replaced: one assistant text block plus one input_image content block, with no MEDIA directive left in visible text.

- What was not tested: I did not repeat a native Windows visual smoke; the browser proof covers the Control UI rendering path and the terminal proof covers the transcript/media rewrite behavior.

target.title: plugin SDK: add inbound_activity hook + discord emit points
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a new plugin hook `inbound_activity` that lets a sibling plugin participate in per-channel inbound debouncing without owning the channel inbound flow. Currently `@openclaw/discord` owns its `createChannelInboundDebouncer` privately; `inbound_claim` only fires *after* the debouncer flushes, which is too late to extend or cancel a pending window. This PR introduces a pre-flush hook with `extend(ms)` / `cancel()` controls and wires Discord to emit through it for `MESSAGE_CREATE`, `TYPING_START`, and `MESSAGE_DELETE`.

The motivating use case is a smart-debounce sibling plugin that:
- extends the pending window when a Discord user is still typing,
- caps total wait via a plugin-side max-window clamp,
- cancels the buffer when the user deletes their pending message before any reply.

## Hook surface

```ts
type PluginHookInboundActivityEvent = {
  source: "message" | "typing" | "delete" | "update";
  key: string;             // per-channel debounce key
  authorId?: string;
  messageId?: string;
  channelId: string;
  timestamp: number;
  raw?: unknown;
};

type PluginHookInboundActivityContext = {
  config?: OpenClawConfig;
  logger?: PluginHookLogger;
  runId?: string;
  sessionKey?: string;
  channelId: string;
  accountId?: string;
  senderId?: string;
  messageId?: string;
  extend: (ms: number) => void;   // fireAt = max(currentFireAt, Date.now() + ms)
  cancel: () => void;             // drops pending buffer for this key
};

type PluginHookInboundActivityResult = Record<never, never> | void;
```

The hook result is intentionally a no-op shape. Caps, windowing, and per-channel state are plugin-side policy — plugins clamp `ms` themselves before calling `extend()`, and the runtime stays out of cross-plugin merge logic.

## What changed

- `src/plugins/hook-types.ts` — new `inbound_activity` entry in `PluginHookName` / `PLUGIN_HOOK_NAMES` / `PluginHookHandlerMap`, plus event/context/result types.
- `src/plugins/hooks.ts` — `runInboundActivity()` on the hook runner (sequential modifying hook, default merge, no shouldStop).
- `src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts` — `createInboundDebouncer` now accepts `buildActivity` / `onActivity` / `onCancel` and exposes `extendKey` / `cancelKey` / `emitActivity`. `extendKey(key, ms)` sets `fireAt = max(currentFireAt, now + ms)`. `cancelKey(key)` drops buffered items and invokes `onCancel(items, key)` for replay-guard cleanup; cleanup errors are swallowed so a release failure can't throw out of the hook path.
- `src/channels/inbound-debounce-policy.ts` — threads the new `buildActivity` / `onActivity` callbacks through `createChannelInboundDebouncer` (and propagates the `TActivity` generic so the returned `emitActivity` types correctly).
- `extensions/discord/src/internal/listeners.ts` — adds `TypingStartListener` and `MessageDeleteListener` base classes.
- `extensions/discord/src/monitor/listeners.ts` — adds `DiscordInboundActivityEmitter`, `DiscordTypingStartListener`, and `DiscordMessageDeleteListener` that emit through the same `emitInboundActivity` entry point. Delete payloads do not include the original author, so deletes use a channel-wildcard key (`discord:<account>:<channel>:*`) that the message handler resolves back to the original per-author key via a small `pendingMessageKeys` map.
- `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.ts` — wires `buildActivity`/`onActivity` for `MESSAGE_CREATE`, exposes `emitInboundActivity` to typing/delete listeners, and adds an `onCancel` callback that releases inbound replay-dedupe entries and prunes `pendingMessageKeys` when a sibling plugin cancels a pending buffer (so a future event for the same message id isn't silently swallowed by the replay guard).
- `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.startup.ts` — registers the typing-start and message-delete listeners.
- `extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.ts` — adds `GuildMessageTyping` and `DirectMessageTyping` to the always-on intent set so `TYPING_START` actually arrives at the new listener (without this, the typing-extend behavior is silently dead).
- `docs/plugins/hooks.md` — documents the hook including the `extend`/`cancel` semantics.

## Design notes

- **No `maxWindowMs` in the hook result.** An earlier draft returned `{ maxWindowMs }` from handlers and merged "smallest cap wins" in the runtime. That made the runtime do plugin bookkeeping and turned the field into a footgun where any future plugin returning it would silently shrink someone else's window. Plugins clamp `ms` themselves before `extend()` instead.
- **Replay-dedupe cleanup on cancel.** Discord claims an inbound replay key (`accountId:channelId:messageId`) before enqueue, normally committed/released during flush. `onCancel` extracts each buffered entry's `replayKey` and calls `releaseDiscordInboundReplay(...)` so a cancelled buffer doesn't leave the dedupe entry pinned.
- **Hook fires after buffer setup.** In `enqueue`, `buildActivity` / `emitActivity` runs *after* the buffer is created or updated so a plugin's `ctx.cancel()` on a first event for a key acts on the live buffer rather than being a silent no-op.
- **`MESSAGE_UPDATE`** is in the event union but unwired. Future work; current consumers don't need it and Discord's edit semantics need a clear policy before opting in.
- **Author ID on delete.** Discord's delete payload doesn't include the author. The message handler maps message-id → debounce-key for pending messages so `emitInboundActivity` can resolve the original key. Deletes for messages that aren't pending fall back to the channel-wildcard key.

## Real behavior proof

Built the patched branch locally (`pnpm install && pnpm run build`) and ran `OpenClaw 2026.5.8 (24d9e16)` under an isolated dev profile against a throwaway Discord bot account in a private guild. A sibling plugin registered an `inbound_activity` handler implementing per-channel smart debounce (typing-extend, max-window cap, cancel-on-delete, bystander filter). All four behaviors were exercised against real Discord gateway traffic. Channel and message IDs redacted as `<test-channel>` / `<msg-N>`. Plugin config: `baseDebounceMs=10000`, `extendOnTypingMs=10000`, `maxWindowMs=40000`. The plugin emits `[smart-debounce] extend` and `[smart-debounce] cancel` lines on every action it takes.

### Typing extends pending flush past base debounce (live Discord output)

Sent a message and typed continuously for ~27 seconds. Each `TYPING_START` (~8s rhythm) pushed `fireAt` forward by 10s. Flush only fired ~10s after typing stopped.

```
10:00:11.266  message → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=    0ms
10:00:13.308  typing  → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed= 2042ms
10:00:21.517  typing  → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=10250ms  ← past base
10:00:29.788  typing  → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=18522ms
10:00:38.130  typing  → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=26864ms
10:00:48ish   flush + agent dispatch
```

### `maxWindowMs` cap clamps the extension (live Discord output)

Continuous typing past the 40s cap. Last typing ping at window-elapsed=34856ms requested +10000ms; plugin clamped via `min(10000, 40000-34856) = 5144`, fired at exactly windowStart+40000ms.

```
10:02:59.769  typing → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=10309ms
10:03:08.035  typing → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=18575ms
10:03:16.188  typing → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=26728ms
10:03:24.315  typing → extend  +5144ms fireAt-in= 5144ms window-elapsed=34856ms  ← clamp
10:03:29.462  flush  (40.002s after the original message)
```

Cap honored to within 2ms.

### Delete cancels the pending buffer (live Discord output)

Sent a message, waited ~4s, deleted it.

```
10:04:44.434  message → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms pending=1
10:04:48.536  cancel  reason=delete-emptied-pending messageId=<msg-C>
```

No subsequent `outcome=completed` for `<msg-C>` — agent never ran. The patch's `onCancel` callback released the inbound replay-dedupe entry. A subsequent message in the same channel processed on a clean window.

### Bystander typing is ignored (live Discord output)

User A sent a message; user B typed in the same channel during the window. The `inbound_activity` hook fired for B's typing, but the plugin's `!pendingAuthors.has(authorId)` guard returned silently — no `extend` log line, flush fired at exact base 10s.

```
10:14:57.871  HOOK fired               ← user A MESSAGE_CREATE
10:14:57.873  PLUGIN extended +10000ms
10:14:58.370  HOOK fired (no extend)   ← user B TYPING_START — ignored
10:14:58.588  HOOK fired (no extend)   ← user B TYPING_START — ignored
10:15:07.875  flush at base 10s
```

Confirms chatty bystanders can't stall agent replies.

### Notes on the test environment

- Patched build: `OpenClaw 2026.5.8 (24d9e16)`, applied cleanly on top of `main`.
- Typing intent fix verified live: with the bot account's privileged `MESSAGE_CONTENT` intent enabled and the new `GuildMessageTyping` / `DirectMessageTyping` intents in `resolveDiscordGatewayIntents`, `TYPING_START` events arrived and dispatched through `inbound_activity` as expected. Without the intent change the typing listener is silently dead.
- One incidental fix landed during local typecheck: `createInboundDebouncer<T>(...)` in `inbound-debounce-policy.ts` was missing its `TActivity` generic; `pnpm run build` failed on TS2322 in `build:plugin-sdk:dts` until that was propagated. Now `<T, TActivity>`.
- Sibling plugin used for these tests is local-only and not part of this PR.

## Test plan

- [x] Build passes (`pnpm install && pnpm run build`) on the patched branch.
- [x] Smoke (real Discord): typing extends past the base debounce — see proof above.
- [x] Smoke (real Discord): max-window cap engages and clamps — see proof above.
- [x] Smoke (real Discord): delete cancels the pending buffer with no agent run — see proof above.
- [x] Smoke (real Discord): bystander typing is ignored — see proof above.
- [x] No behavior change when no plugin registers `inbound_activity` (early-return on `hasHooks("inbound_activity")` keeps the existing path identical — confirmed by reading the dispatch path).

target.title: Add snapshot SQLite stress harness

Depends on #94805.RFC: openclaw/rfcs#20Related: #94675, #94646## Summary- Adds an opt-in SQLite snapshot stress harness for the core `snapshot` work.- Exercises snapshot create/copy/restore while a concurrent writer commits transaction batches into the same source SQLite database.- Covers both Phase 1 DB units: `--target global` and `--agent <id>`.- Keeps writer lifecycle monitoring attached for the full snapshot loop so worker result/error/exit states fail deterministically instead of being missed.- Supports repeated runs against the same `--state-dir` by continuing batch IDs and using run-scoped sync/restore scratch paths.- Adds package scripts for smoke/default/large profiles.## ScopeThis is measurement and validation plumbing only. It does not add WAL bundles, scheduling, object storage, failover orchestration, or CI-gated long stress runs.## Real behavior proofBehavior or issue addressed:Core snapshot artifacts need a repeatable stress harness that proves restored safe-sync files remain SQLite-valid and do not expose partial writer transactions while the source DB is being mutated. This update also addresses review findings by keeping a single writer monitor attached from start through stop and making documented `--state-dir` reuse repeatable.Real environment tested:Windows checkout at `giodl/snapshot-stress-pr5` (`50bc0b102e`), stacked on PR #94805 head `86f92a1e70`, Node v22.22.3, source harness using `node:sqlite`, OpenClaw local SQLite state databases, and the stacked core snapshot provider from this PR stack. Node prints the expected experimental warning for `node:sqlite`.Exact steps or command run after this patch:1. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts`2. `pnpm exec oxlint scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.ts test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts`3. `pnpm exec oxfmt --check scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.ts scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.mjs test/scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.test.ts package.json`4. `git diff --check`5. test:snapshot:stress`7. `pnpm test:snapshot:stress:large`8. `node --import tsx scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.mjs --profile default --agent main --output .artifacts/snapshot-stress/agent-default.json`9. `node --import tsx scripts/bench-snapshot-sqlite.mjs --profile large --agent main --output .artifacts/snapshot-stress/agent-large.json`Evidence after target.title: perf: optimize agent tool result middleware runtime check
target.body:
Replaces an $O(N \times M)$ array lookup with an $O(N + M)$ Set-based lookup when filtering runtimes against declared middleware in the plugin registry. This improves efficiency during plugin registration.

## Summary

- Problem: Suboptimal O(N*M) data structure inside an array filter in the plugin registry, and CI failures in GitHub Actions workflows when secrets are missing.
- Why it matters: Improves performance during plugin registration and ensures CI stability for external contributors/forks.
- What changed: 
    - Optimized `src/plugins/registry.ts` to use a `Set` for runtime lookups.
    - Updated `auto-response.yml`, `labeler.yml`, and `stale.yml` to gracefully handle missing App secrets.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Core plugin registration logic and workflow automation functionality remains identical.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes # (N/A)
- Related # (N/A)
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression (CI failure)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Manual logic review of YAML changes and code review of TS changes.
- Edge cases checked: Empty secrets in workflows, small vs large arrays in registry.
- What you did **not** verify: Actual execution of GitHub Actions (not possible in sandbox).

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes` - added guards for empty secrets)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Changes only affect how CI handles missing secrets; it falls back to standard `github.token` which is safer for public forks.

target.title: Add core SQLite snapshot command
target.body:
## SummaryThis PR adds a core SQLite snapshot command for OpenClaw-owned state.It includes:- shared SQLite snapshot provider and local artifact repository- `openclaw snapshot create|verify|restore|list`- named OpenClaw database targets: `--target global` and `--agent <id>`- explicit `--db <path>` support for advanced/manual SQLite files- realpath resolution for named targets and explicit `--db` paths before snapshotting- global-state artifact sanitization for transient `delivery_queue_entries`, matching the backup recovery invariant- restore cleanup for stale `-wal`, `-shm`, and `-journal` sidecars- docs for which files are safe to sync, which live SQLite sidecars should be ignored, and why snapshot artifacts must still be protected as sensitive state- docs that distinguish `openclaw snapshot` from `openclaw backup``openclaw snapshot` is the narrow per-database SQLite sync artifact. `openclaw backup` remains the broader `.tar.gz` recovery archive for state, config, credentials, sessions, and optional workspaces.## WhyThe goal from #94675 is not to make hosts copy live SQLite files more carefully. The goal is for OpenClaw to produce a SQLite-safe sync artifact.This PR establishes that boundary:- live `.sqlite`, `-wal`, `-shm`, and `-journal` files remain hot local runtime state- `openclaw snapshot create` materializes a portable artifact directory containing `manifest.json` and `database.sqlite`- hosts, containers, object-store sync jobs, and backup systems should sync the completed snapshot directory, not the live SQLite file family- restore hydrates a fresh local SQLite file from the verified artifact before OpenClaw opens itThe Lobster-shaped host case is covered by this contract: hosts ask OpenClaw for named database targets and sync completed artifacts. This PR intentionally does not add a `memory-search` target, because memory-search currently shares the per-agent database and a memory-only export needs a separate design.## StackThis PR combines the previous core implementation slices:- former #94694: core snapshot provider proof- former #94717: core `openclaw snapshot` CLI- former #94799: named OpenClaw database targets- this PR: safe-sync artifact, restore proof/docs, realpath handling, and recovery invariantsFollow-up validation PR: #94967RFC: openclaw/rfcs#20Related: #94675, #94646## ScopeThis does not add memory-search-only export, WAL bundle deltas, object storage upload, scheduling, leases, failover automation, restore-on-boot behavior, production orchestration, or a new memory-search path config. Those remain future phases or separate maintainer decisions. This PR ships the local core command and artifact contract.## Real behavior proofBehavior or issue addressed:OpenClaw can convert OpenClaw-owned SQLite state into syncable artifact directories for global and per-agent databases, verify them, copy them independently from live SQLite files, and restore them into verified fresh SQLite databases. Global snapshot artifacts also scrub transient delivery queue rows, and restore removes stale SQLite sidecars before opening the restored database.Real environment tested:Windows checkout at `C:\src\openclaw-snapshot-pr1`, PR head `86f92a1e70`, Node v22.22.3, `pnpm build` packaged output, `node openclaw.mjs` launcher commands, temporary local SQLite databases created with Node `node:sqlite`, local snapshot repository under `C:\tmp\openclaw-snapshot-final-proof`, plus source Vitest harnesses with WAL-mode SQLite databases, named OpenClaw database targets, realpath coverage, global transient queue scrubbing, rollback journal cleanup, and config schema validation.Exact steps or command run after this patch:1. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/commands/snapshot.test.ts src/snapshot/snapshot-provider.test.ts src/cli/program/register.snapshot.test.ts src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts`2. `pnpm exec oxfmt --check docs/cli/snapshot.md src/commands/snapshot.ts src/commands/snapshot.test.ts src/snapshot/local-repository.ts src/snapshot/snapshot-provider.ts src/snapshot/snapshot-provider.test.ts src/cli/program/register.snapshot.ts src/cli/program/register.snapshot.test.ts src/agents/memory-search.ts src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts src/config/schema.help.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts src/config/types.tools.ts src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts`3. `git diff --check`4. `pnpm build`5. `codex review --base origin/main`6. Packaged `node openclaw.mjs snapshot create|verify|restore` proof for canonical global and per-agent databases from the built launcher.Evidence after fix:Focused validation passed 5 Vitest shards:- `src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts`: 35 tests- `src/snapshot/snapshot-provider.test.ts`: 6 tests- `src/agents/memory-search.test.ts`: 34 tests- `src/cli/program/register.snapshot.test.ts`: 7 tests- `src/commands/snapshot.test.ts`: 9 tests`pnpm build` passed at `86f92a1e70`.`codex review --base origin/main` returned:```textNo actionable regressions were found in the snapshot command, provider, manifest validation, or SQLite snapshot helper paths. target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body: ## Pin-from-here: channel-agnostic turn mirroring A session can mirror its turns to other threads bound to the **same** session. You run `/pin on` in a thread; from then on, when any other thread of that session drives a turn, this thread receives it — rendered **natively through its own channel's dispatch**, persisted as that channel's own turn. ### Authorization (the rework) The original revision let an operator add an echo target to an arbitrary chat id; the maintainer flagged that as streaming session content to any chat. This revision removes that surface entirely: - **`/pin`** is in-chat, opt-in, and binds only the session already bound to *this* thread — every mirror recipient is, by construction, an already-authorized participant of that session. There is no "stream to an arbitrary chat id" path. - Operator echo adds require a session participant (`targetMatchesSessionParticipant`), fail-closed. ### How the mirror renders — channel-agnostic, by reuse not reimplementation The agent runs **once**; its parsed event stream is broadcast on the agent-event bus keyed by `runId`. For each pinned target, the core hands that target's **own channel dispatch** a bus-sourced `replyResolver` (`createMirrorReplyResolver`) that replays the origin run's stream instead of calling the model: - `dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher` already accepts a pluggable `replyResolver`. `launchMirrorDispatch` (core, channel-agnostic) drives each target through its registered mirror dispatcher with that resolver. - Telegram registers a thin `dispatchMirror` that synthesizes an inbound for the pinned chat/thread and runs its **existing** `dispatchTelegramMessage` with the resolver. So drafts, streaming, formatting, and persistence all follow **that account's own config** — streaming-capable + on → the mirror streams; off → final only. Nothing is forced; nothing is reimplemented per channel. - **Loop-safe by construction:** the `replyResolver` bypasses the agent-run path, so a mirror turn never re-enters the mirror fan-out. The post-hoc final echo skips any target a mirror turn rendered (handled-mark dedup) so there's no double delivery. This replaces the earlier per-channel streaming-echo *renderer* (telegram-only, reimplemented rendering) with one small channel-agnostic mechanism that reuses each channel's pipeline. **Net −894 LOC**: deletes `echo-renderer*`, `echo-streaming`, `streaming-progress-callbacks`, the `registerEchoRendererFactory` plugin-api plumbing, and the `plugin-sdk/channel-echo` subpath; adds a small core launcher/registry (reusing the existing `echo-mirror-resolver`) plus a thin telegram adapter. ### Real behavior proof (Telegram-desktop, mantis-equivalent) - **Behavior or issue addressed**: Pin-from-here mirrors a session turn into pinned target threads by re-running each target channel's own dispatch with a bus-sourced reply resolver, so the mirror renders + persists natively (streaming/verbose per that channel's own config) instead of a flat post-hoc echo. - **Real environment tested**: A real OpenClaw gateway built from this branch in a Linux crabbox with a desktop; a real Telegram bot + a real Telegram account on Telegram Desktop; a real `claude-cli` backend (`sonnet-4-6`/`opus-4-8`, live Anthropic API) with exec tools; the tdlib user-driver sending real messages. This recreates the Mantis telegram-desktop proof flow locally (same mechanism: telegram-desktop + user-driver + screen capture). - **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Started the gateway from the built branch; in a Telegram thread sent `/new`, `/verbose on`, `/pin on`; then drove the same session from webchat (`chat.send`) with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname`, and a two-step `date -u` + `uname -a` narrated before each command); screen-recorded Telegram Desktop and captured the gateway debug log. - **Evidence after fix**: Direct after-fix artifacts — mirror-render screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-mirror-dispatch.png), multi-account screenshot [`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount.png), and the full **destination-revocation run + gateway log** [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The revocation log shows the mirror deliver when enabled (`messageId=0` synthetic inbound + `outbound send ok chatId=…`), then `[outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed … (destination disabled/revoked)` after the group is disabled. - **Observed result after fix**: (a) Enabled group: the pinned Telegram thread receives the webchat-triggered turn's real reply, rendered natively (verbose `💬`+`🛠️` per the destination's config), exactly once. (b) **Disabled (revoked) group**: zero sends to the group and the tdlib driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE` — neither the native mirror nor the echo fallback delivers. Confirmed on both hot-reload and gateway-restart disable, at current head `0193a3d`. - **What was not tested**: Nothing material remains unproven on current head. **Destination revocation is now proven live** (and a real gap it surfaced is fixed — see *Destination revocation* below). Multi-account routing is also proven on current head — see below. #### Multi-account routing — current head (account-aware dispatch) Two real Telegram accounts in one gateway: `default` = bot #2 in GID `-1003900553563`, `acct2` = bot #3 (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot`) in a separate forum group GID2 `-1004389798714` (bot #3 promoted admin; bot #2 is **not** a member of GID2). The echo target for GID2 is set with `accountId: acct2`, then a **webchat-origin** turn drives the session. Gateway debug log (`.proof/pin-from-here-multiaccount-log.txt`): ```text [telegram] [acct2] starting provider (@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot) [telegram] [default] starting provider (@JeeevesOpenClawTelegram2bot) message received: channel=webchat ... sessionKey=agent:main:telegram:group:-1004389798714:topic:1 message dispatch started: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver ← the mirror message dispatch completed: channel=telegram ... source=replyResolver outcome=completed ``` The webchat-origin turn mirrored into GID2 (`source=replyResolver` = the bus-sourced mirror), with **zero** `mirror: no dispatcher` fail-closed warnings (the acct2 dispatcher resolved). Because **bot #2 is not in GID2** (`getChatMember` → `chat not found`), a successful mirror delivery there could *only* be the **acct2** account (bot #3) — proving the mirror routes through the target's own account, not the first-registered/default one. #### Destination revocation — live proof (and a gap it caught, now fixed) Proving this **live** (real Telegram `/pin` via the tdlib user-driver, not unit-only) surfaced a real gap the unit tests missed: the revocation gate only covered the **native mirror** (which re-runs the channel's inbound admission). The prompt / post-hoc **echo** path (`fireEchoDeliveries`) delivers through the *channel-agnostic* raw send, which has **no** admission gate — so a disabled pinned group kept receiving echoes, and when the mirror fan-out didn't fire (e.g. right after the channel restart that disabling a group triggers) the post-hoc echo leaked the conversation to the revoked destination. **Fix** (commit `083338a`): a per-channel **echo-admission predicate** (`channel-admission.ts`), registered per account exactly like the mirror dispatcher (last-wins, unregister on stop, **fail closed**). `fireEchoDeliveries` filters targets through it; telegram registers a predicate that reports group/topic-disabled from its live config. Now a disabled destination gets **neither** the native mirror **nor** the echo fallback. **Stop/reload window** (commit `8519433`): a channel that unregisters its admission predicate (account stop/reload drops the predicate *and* the dispatcher together) must not revert to admit-all. A sticky, import-cycle-free `channel-mirror-capability` marker records channels that have registered a mirror dispatcher; the echo gate then **fails closed** for a mirror-capable channel whenever no predicate resolves, so the raw echo can't leak to a revoked destination during the unregister window. Non-mirror channels admit echoes unchanged. Unit-covered (`channel-admission.test.ts`). **Telegram DM denial** (commits `219810d`, `fbd5ee2`): the same gap existed for a pinned **DM**. Three parts: (a) `enforceTelegramDmAccess` denial returned null *without* the revocation marker → `dispatchMirror` threw → unmark → raw echo could reach the denied DM; now a denied DM in a mirror context calls `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked` (keep the mark, suppress the post-hoc). (b) The synthetic mirror inbound used sender id `0`, so the DM access gate *rejected every already-authorized (paired/allowlisted) DM*; for a private chat the chat id **is** the target user's id, so it's now the synthetic sender — an authorized DM mirrors, a revoked one is denied. (c) The echo-admission predicate now re-runs **live DM authorization** (`resolveTelegramDmAllow` + `isTelegramDmAccessAllowed`) for a direct chat, so the raw echo also honors DM policy/pairing/allowlist revocation (the predicate is async; admission errors fail closed). Unit-covered: `bot-message-context.mirror.test.ts` (DM-denial → `onMirrorAdmissionBlocked`), `channel-admission.test.ts` (async predicate + fail-closed), `echo.test.ts` (async suppression). **Live-proven** (real DM: driver/Cameron DMs bot #2, `/pin on` in the DM): with `dmPolicy: open` the authorized DM mirrors (validating the sender-identity fix); after `dmPolicy: disabled` the DM gets **zero** sends, the driver observing it reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`, and the gateway logs `Echo delivery suppressed … telegram:8661849123 (destination disabled/revoked)` — see the *DIRECT-DM REVOCATION* section of [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). **Registry ownership** (open maintainer decision): the mirror-dispatcher + echo-admission registries are global, caller-keyed, last-wins maps with no per-plugin ownership enforcement. They are exported from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound` and **only the bundled telegram channel uses them**. Whether to keep them as a public seam, hand them out through an owner-scoped registrar, or keep them internal is a maintainer API call — flagged explicitly in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md`. (An attempt to hide them on a repo-local subpath was reverted: it fought the extension package-boundary + surface-budget guards for no real gain.) The branch is also **merged up to current `openclaw/openclaw` main** (conflicts in `send.ts` / `agent-runner-execution.ts` resolved). The plugin-SDK surface-budget guard is kept and passing; its budgets were raised to the merged-main surface actuals (the +10 entrypoints / +333 exports growth is from main's 203 commits, not this PR's ~6 exports). **Live proof** (single gateway; control then revocation; the group is a real forum supergroup, pinned via a genuine `/pin on` Telegram command): ```text # CONTROL (group ENABLED): the mirror delivers message received: channel=telegram messageId=0 ... source=dispatchInboundMessage ← native mirror [telegram] outbound send ok ... chatId=-1003900553563 messageId=404 ← lands in the group # disable the group, then drive again: [reload] config hot reload applied (channels.telegram.groups.-1003900553563.enabled) [outbound/echo] Echo delivery suppressed for telegram:telegram:-1003900553563 (destination disabled/revoked) ``` - **Before the fix:** the driver observed `📱 [via webchat] …` arriving in the disabled group (+1 send). - **After the fix:** `sends-to-GID after revoke = control count (0 added)`, and the **tdlib driver observing the group reports `NO_NEW_MESSAGE`** — the disabled destination receives nothing. Confirmed on **both** revocation paths: config hot-reload **and** a full gateway restart. Unit coverage: `channel-admission.test.ts` (registry + fail-closed) and an `echo.test.ts` suppression case. Full redacted run + gateway log for both paths: [`.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt`](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/proof/pin-from-here-evidence/.proof/pin-from-here-revocation.txt). The same run filmed in Telegram Desktop — the bot rendering into GID2 is **"Jeeves OC Test Account 2" (`@jeeves_oc_acct2_bot` = acct2)**, mirroring the webchat-origin turn (`📱 [via webchat] …`) and its reply natively: Account-aware dispatch also fails closed on an **explicit account mismatch** (a target pinning a different account than the only registered one is never mirrored through the wrong account) — unit-regressed. Recreation of the Mantis telegram-desktop flow on a local patched crabbox: a real gateway built from this branch (current `openclaw/openclaw` main + this work), Telegram Desktop + the user-driver + screen capture, driven by a **real `claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6` backend with an exec tool call** (not a mock). A Telegram thread runs `/pin on`; webchat then drives the same session with a tool-using prompt (`sleep 3; hostname` — output not derivable from context). The pinned thread renders the **webchat-origin turn's reply natively** — including the real tool result — through telegram's own dispatch: 1. `/verbose on` → "⚙️ Verbose logging enabled." 2. `/pin on` → **"📌 Pinned. This thread will mirror turns from other threads of this session."** 3. `📱 [via webchat] Use your exec (bash) tool to run … sleep 3; hostname …` 4. The pinned thread receives the real reply: **"The hostname is `crabbox-golden-crayfish-fa445ed1`."** — the actual box hostname, i.e. a real `hostname` exec run by the webchat-triggered turn, rendered natively in the pinned Telegram thread via the channel-agnostic mirror dispatch. Mechanism confirmed in the gateway log for the filmed run: `launchMirrorDispatch` fires for the webchat-origin turn (origin = webchat, telegram target not self-excluded, dispatcher found); the telegram `dispatchMirror` builds the synthetic inbound (`context=BUILT`) and runs `dispatchTelegramMessage` for the full turn (`message processed: channel=telegram messageId=0 … completed`); exactly one reply lands (post-hoc assistant echo skipped by the handled-mark, no duplicate). #### Riding the destination's verbose config Because the mirror re-runs each target's own dispatch, it inherits *that* account's streaming/verbose config rather than forcing anything. With the daily driver's setup (`verboseDefault: on`, `streaming.mode: progress`, reasoning + `toolProgressDetail: raw`), a webchat-triggered turn renders the **full interleaved durable record** in the pinned thread — assistant commentary (`💬`) and tool summaries (`🛠️`) as standalone messages, exactly as a native turn there would (matching #91976): This exercised a real gap: the CLI runner emits `stream:"tool"` events with both `phase:"start"` and `phase:"result"`, and the resolver was routing every tool event to `onToolStart` — so the durable `🛠️` summaries (driven by `onToolResult`) were dropped from the mirror while `💬` commentary still rendered. Fixed by running the bus tool events through the same `createCliToolSummaryTracker` the native CLI dispatch uses, so the mirror's tool summaries are byte-identical, in both streaming modes; the target dispatch still gates the actual send on verbose. ### Public SDK contract The mirror-dispatcher seam (`registerChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `unregisterChannelMirrorDispatcher` / `MirrorDispatcher`) is now **documented** as a public `channel-outbound` contract in `docs/plugins/sdk-overview.md` (new *Channel mirror dispatcher* section): per-account registration, the last-wins reload lifecycle, and the account-match + destination-revocation behavior a channel plugin must honor (the native dispatcher is the sole delivery authority, so it must run the channel's own admission/revocation path). The barrel export points at that section. It stays on its neutral SDK subpath per the repo's internal-module convention rather than being reached into as an internal — final acceptance of the public surface is the maintainers' call. ### Tests + CI - `tsgo:core`: clean. Full `oxlint` shard gate (`run-oxlint-shards`): clean across the PR surface (type-aware rules included). - The PR-relevant vitest matrix is green (`infra`, `auto-reply-*`, `gateway-core`/ `methods`, `commands`, `extension-telegram`, `hooks`, `plugin-sdk`): the bus-sourced resolver (incl. the tool-summary replay), handled-mark dedup, and `/pin` fan-out are covered. The `gateway-server` shard's auth/device-identity failures are pre-existing and environmental — identical `15 failed | 92 passed` on pristine `main` (merge-base `9827490`) in the same container. ### AI assistance Built with AI assistance (Claude). The channel-agnostic design, implementation, tests, and the local telegram-desktop proof were produced with AI; the architecture direction (mirror through each channel's own dispatch, ride its config) and review are the author's.

commands.author: heichaowo

target.title: fix(telegram): skip mirror write when primary reply already exists (#94930)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94930.

Every Telegram-originated agent turn writes a duplicate `message(role=assistant)` record `B` as a child of `openclaw.cache-ttl` ~1s after the primary reply `A`. `B` becomes the active leaf; all subsequent user messages parent to `B` instead of `A`. `A` becomes an orphaned branch, causing:

- Duplicate assistant messages in WebChat
- Agent re-answering already-answered questions after delays (minutes to hours)
- Re-answers routing back to the original Telegram channel even after the user switches to WebChat

**Observed on v2026.6.8 — 100% reproducible from Telegram, not reproducible from WebChat native.**

## Root cause

Two writers touch the session transcript per Telegram turn:

- **A**: embedded runner (normal agent execution path — `appendAssistantMessageToSessionTranscript`)
- **B**: `mirrorTelegramAssistantReplyToTranscript()` in `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts` — calls `appendSessionTranscriptMessage()` **directly**, bypassing the facade and its delivery-mirror dedup helpers

After prompt+compaction, `attempt.ts` appends an `openclaw.cache-ttl` custom node which becomes the active leaf. ~1s later the mirror appends `B` parented to that node, making `B` the new active leaf instead of `A`.

`appendSessionTranscriptMessageLocked` has an idempotency check via `readMessageIdempotencyKey`, but the mirror writer's constructed message has **no `idempotencyKey`** field — the check returns `undefined` and is skipped, so `B` is written unconditionally.

**Note:** commit `f5eddc2b6d` patched `findLatestEquivalentAssistantMessageId` in `transcript.ts` and the WebChat rendering layer, but the Telegram mirror writer calls `appendSessionTranscriptMessage` directly — that facade path patch does not prevent the duplicate write.

## Fix

In `mirrorTelegramAssistantReplyToTranscript()` (`extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts`), before calling `appendSessionTranscriptMessage`, read the transcript tail via `readTailAssistantTextFromSessionTranscript` and compare its text to the mirror text. If they match, skip the mirror write.

`readTail` (unlike the previous `readLatest`) stops scanning at the first user-role line it encounters, so the comparison is strictly bounded to the **current turn**. This closes the false-negative that `readLatest` had: after a "say it again" turn that produces the same text as a prior turn, `readLatest` would match the *previous* turn's identical reply and wrongly skip a legitimate mirror write — losing a transcript row. `readTail` cannot cross a user line, so it only matches evidence from the turn that just completed.

This approach also preserves the existing dedup behavior for the `message_tool`-only path (where the primary runner writes nothing): in that case `readTail` returns `null` and the mirror write proceeds normally.

Changes (4 files, +70/−5):
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts` — swap reader + concise 3-line invariant comment
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.runtime.ts` + `src/plugin-sdk/session-store-runtime.ts` — re-export `readTailAssistantTextFromSessionTranscript` as a named export on the existing subpath (`pnpm build`'s `check-plugin-sdk-exports` passed)
- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts` — added regression test (prior identical assistant → user line → new mirror ⇒ writes, not skipped), plus tail-reader mock wiring

## Testing

`pnpm test extensions/telegram` → **2162 passed, 0 failed**

## Real behavior proof

### Issue addressed

Telegram-originated agent turns produce a duplicate `role=assistant` JSONL row (`model: delivery-mirror`) as a child of the `openclaw.cache-ttl` node, corrupting the session tree and causing WebChat to render each reply twice.

### Environment tested

Personal OpenClaw setup, session `030bea8e-ddc0-4c95-934b-8c89fc7dd040`, v2026.6.8, macOS, Telegram bot channel → WebChat cross-channel.

### Exact steps or command run after the patch

1. Send a message to the OpenClaw agent via Telegram bot
2. Open the same session in WebChat
3. Observe that only one assistant bubble appears (no duplicate)
4. Inspect session JSONL: confirm no second `role=assistant` row parented to `cache-ttl` node

### Evidence after fix

Before fix — live JSONL from session `030bea8e` (reproduced prior to this patch):

```
14:25:09 message id=95dbc41a par=74d45537 role=assistant ← A (primary reply, embedded runner)
14:25:09 custom id=bfa1edcb par=95dbc41a kind=cache-ttl ← cache-ttl inserted as active leaf
14:25:10 message id=8d1b4c19 par=bfa1edcb role=assistant ← B (delivery-mirror duplicate)
14:26:33 message id=c3f2a801 par=8d1b4c19 role=user ← next user msg parents to B, not A
```

Full evidence with tree diagrams: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/94930#issuecomment-4752524069

After fix — live JSONL from the same session with patch applied to the installed dist, Telegram-originated turn (message: "测试"), 2026-06-20T03:12 GMT+8:

```
custom id=2aaf2f5f par=918f1b2d customType=openclaw.cache-ttl
message id=3d4659ae par=2aaf2f5f role=user
message id=816e1778 par=3d4659ae role=assistant model=claude-sonnet-4-6 ← A (primary, no B follows)
message id=f571e312 par=816e1778 role=toolResult
message id=debd14fc par=f571e312 role=assistant model=claude-sonnet-4-6
```

No `model=delivery-mirror` row appears after the `cache-ttl` node. `mirrorTelegramAssistantReplyToTranscript` read the transcript tail, found the primary reply text matched, and returned early without writing.

### Observed result

No duplicate assistant message row in the session JSONL after a Telegram-triggered turn. WebChat renders one bubble per turn. Session tree corruption eliminated.

commands.author: RomneyDa

target.title: feat(qa): crabline channel driver
target.body:
## Summary

This PR makes QA profile channel drivers executable and separates channel identity from transport mode.

- Adds the merged `crabline` package as a QA Lab dependency pinned to `openclaw/crabline@93b4de9b933b2c32f16f265725a0af3c429997e2`.
- Runs the `smoke-ci` QA profile through the Crabline runtime via `import("crabline")`, with `channel: telegram` and other requested channel IDs resolved through Crabline instead of an inlined local fallback.
- Adds a separate `QA Smoke CI` PR workflow for the canonical mock-channel smoke profile.
- Keeps `.github/workflows/qa-live-transports-convex.yml` focused on scheduled/manual live transport coverage.
- Makes the `release` live profile fan out to the native Matrix, Telegram, Discord, Slack, and WhatsApp live lane catalogs, then merges their `qa-evidence.json` outputs into one profile evidence artifact.

## Why

Tests should be able to ask for a channel shape such as `telegram` while the selected profile decides whether that exchange is handled by Crabline or by a real live transport. `smoke-ci` is the PR CI mock-channel profile. Live service coverage stays in the live transport lanes and release profile evidence.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check .github/workflows/qa-live-transports-convex.yml .github/workflows/qa-smoke-ci.yml docs/concepts/qa-e2e-automation.md extensions/qa-lab/package.json pnpm-workspace.yaml extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-channel-driver.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-channel-driver.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-transport.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-transport.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/suite.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec tsgo --noEmit -p tsconfig.extensions.json`
- `pnpm vitest run extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-transport.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-channel-driver.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/suite.test.ts test/scripts/package-acceptance-workflow.test.ts`

commands.author: giodl73-repo

target.title: Doctor: expose session lock findings
target.body:
## Summary

- Exposes stale session write locks as structured doctor health findings.
- Adds dry-run repair effects for stale lock removal.
- Keeps the existing session-lock note/repair function authoritative for real normal `doctor` / `doctor --fix` behavior.
- Wires the structured check through the existing ordered doctor contribution path.

This is intentionally the session-lock slice from the doctor repair stack. It does not change session directory discovery, lock ownership detection, or the real mutating cleanup path.

## Stack

Stacked on #86627, current base head `bd1000b409d`. The only new session-lock commit in this PR is signed head `3fea011755`; the session-lock delta is 6 files.

## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/commands/doctor-lint.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-locks.ts src/commands/doctor-session-locks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/commands/doctor-lint.test.ts src/commands/doctor-session-locks.ts src/commands/doctor-session-locks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run --project commands src/commands/doctor-session-locks.test.ts` (7 tests)
- `pnpm exec vitest run --project commands src/commands/doctor-lint.test.ts` (9 tests)
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts` (14 tests)
- `pnpm exec vitest run --project unit-fast src/flows/doctor-health-contributions.test.ts` (39 tests)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Stale session lock files are reported through `doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/session-locks`, and structured repair previews no longer claim that report-only live OpenClaw-owned locks would be removed. Locks whose stale reasons are only `too-old` / `hold-exceeded` now preview as preserved; removable stale locks still preview as removal.

Real environment tested:
WSL2 Ubuntu 24.04, source checkout `/root/src/openclaw-84366-proof` at signed head `650f6c09d4` (`fix(doctor): preserve report-only session locks in preview`). The CLI proof used an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, and `HOME`. The preview-effect proof used a source harness importing `src/commands/doctor-session-locks.ts` with injected owner-process args so the live OpenClaw-owned lock case is deterministic.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. Created isolated temp state under `/tmp/openclaw-84366-proof-*` with `state/agents/main/sessions/*.jsonl.lock` fixtures.
2. Ran `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<temp>/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<temp>/state/openclaw.json HOME=<temp>/home COREPACK_HOME=/root/.cache/node/corepack OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/session-locks`.
3. Ran `node --import tsx --input-type=module` source harness importing `detectStaleSessionLocks` and `sessionLockToRepairEffect` from `src/commands/doctor-session-locks.ts` against the same isolated state.
4. Ran focused validation: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/doctor-session-locks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts --reporter=dot`; `pnpm exec oxlint src/commands/doctor-session-locks.ts src/commands/doctor-session-locks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts`; `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/commands/doctor-session-locks.ts src/commands/doctor-session-locks.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts`; `git diff --check`.

Evidence after fix:
CLI lint output returned nonzero as expected for warnings and reported one selected check:

```json
{
  "cliStatus": 1,
  "cliOk": false,
  "checksRun": 1,
  "checksSkipped": 22,
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "core/doctor/session-locks",
      "severity": "warning",
      "message": "Stale session lock file: /tmp/openclaw-84366-proof-*/state/agents/main/sessions/bash-owner.jsonl.lock (non-openclaw-owner)",
      "fixHint": "Run \"openclaw doctor --fix\" to remove stale lock files automatically.",
      "pathBasename": "bash-owner.jsonl.lock"
    },
    {
      "checkId": "core/doctor/session-locks",
      "severity": "warning",
      "message": "Stale session lock file: /tmp/openclaw-84366-proof-*/state/agents/main/sessions/dead-owner.jsonl.lock (missing-pid)",
      "fixHint": "Run \"openclaw doctor --fix\" to remove stale lock files automatically.",
      "pathBasename": "dead-owner.jsonl.lock"
    }
  ]
}
```

Source preview-effect harness output showed the fixed distinction:

```json
{
  "summary": [
    {
      "file": "report-only.jsonl.lock",
      "staleReasons": ["too-old"],
      "effect": {
        "kind": "state",
        "action": "would-preserve-report-only-stale-session-lock",
        "dryRunSafe": false
      }
    },
    {
      "file": "dead-owner-harness.jsonl.lock",
      "staleReasons": ["missing-pid", "too-old"],
      "effect": {
        "kind": "state",
        "action": "would-remove-stale-session-lock",
        "dryRunSafe": false
      }
    }
  ],
  "reportOnlyStillExists": true,
  "removableStillExists": true
}
```

Focused validation passed: session-lock Vitest shard 14 tests passed; core-check Vitest shard 8 tests passed; targeted `oxlint` found 0 warnings/errors; `oxfmt --check` passed; `git diff --check` was clean.

Observed result after fix:
`doctor --lint --json --only core/doctor/session-locks` surfaces structured stale-lock warnings from the isolated source CLI run. The patched repair-effect path preserves report-only live OpenClaw-owned stale locks in preview output while continuing to mark missing-pid stale locks as removable. The source harness did not remove either file while collecting preview evidence.

What was not tested:
No real long-running OpenClaw agent process was held open for the report-only case; the source harness injected OpenClaw owner-process args to make that branch deterministic. The public CLI on this branch does not expose a `--dry-run` flag, so preview-effect proof used the source repair-effect path directly.
